<!-- Topic 2: Vectors of Objects -->
<!-- Total slides: 5 -->

# Vectors of Objects

---

## Why a vector beats a static array for objects {.smaller}

+ A static array of objects requires the size at compile time — you must know upfront how many objects you need.
+ A `vector<ClassName>` grows as you add objects and exposes `.size()`, `.push_back()`, and the full STL algorithm set.

::: notes
Total slides: 5
Students know vectors from week 2 and static arrays from week 11. The connection here is mechanical: swap ClassName[] for vector<ClassName>. The loop patterns, dot-notation access, and method calls are identical — only the container changes. Emphasize: vector is almost always the right choice unless you have a hard size constraint or performance requirement.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Declaring and adding objects {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

class Student {
public:
    string name;
    int    grade = 0;
    void   setName(string n)  { name = n; }
    void   setGrade(int g)    { grade = g; }
    string getName()  const   { return name; }
    int    getGrade() const   { return grade; }
};

int main() {
    vector<Student> roster;        // no size needed at declaration

    Student s;
    s.setName("Alice");  s.setGrade(92);  roster.push_back(s);
    s.setName("Bob");    s.setGrade(85);  roster.push_back(s);
    s.setName("Carol");  s.setGrade(97);  roster.push_back(s);

    cout << "Roster size: " << roster.size() << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Looping — index vs. range-for {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
// Index loop — use when you need the position
for (int i = 0; i < roster.size(); i++)
    cout << i + 1 << ". " << roster[i].getName()
         << " — " << roster[i].getGrade() << "\n";

// Range-for — use when you only need the value
for (const Student& s : roster)
    cout << s.getName() << ": " << s.getGrade() << "\n";
```

::: notes
const Student& avoids copying the object on each iteration — important habit when objects are larger than primitives. The & is "pass by reference inside the loop"; const ensures the loop body cannot accidentally modify roster elements.
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Finding the top student {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
// Max-tracker with a vector — same logic as with a static array
int best = 0;
for (int i = 1; i < roster.size(); i++)
    if (roster[i].getGrade() > roster[best].getGrade()) best = i;

cout << "Top student: " << roster[best].getName()
     << " with " << roster[best].getGrade() << "\n";

// Dynamic growth: read from user until "done"
string name;
while (cin >> name && name != "done") {
    int g;  cin >> g;
    Student tmp;  tmp.setName(name);  tmp.setGrade(g);
    roster.push_back(tmp);
}
```

::: notes
The max-tracker code is identical to the static array version — only the declaration changed. The second block shows the real advantage of vector: students can enter as many records as they want without pre-allocating a size.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Summary {.smaller}

+ `vector<ClassName>` grows dynamically — declare it empty, add objects with `.push_back()`.
+ Access elements the same way as a static array: `vec[i].method()` or `for (const ClassName& obj : vec)`.

<!-- Slide 5 -->
